title: Neuro-Spectral Architectures for Causal Physics-Informed Networks
abstract:
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have emerged as a powerful frame-
work for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). However, standard MLP-
based PINNs often fail to converge when dealing with complex initial value
problems, leading to solutions that violate causality and suffer from a spectral
bias towards low-frequency components. To address these issues, we introduce
NeuSA (Neuro-Spectral Architectures), a novel class of PINNs inspired by classi-
cal spectral methods, designed to solve linear and nonlinear PDEs with variable
coefficients. NeuSA learns a projection of the underlying PDE onto a spectral
basis, leading to a finite-dimensional representation of the dynamics which is then
integrated with an adapted Neural ODE (NODE). This allows us to overcome
spectral bias, by leveraging the high-frequency components enabled by the spectral
representation; to enforce causality, by inheriting the causal structure of NODEs,
and to start training near the target solution, by means of an initialization scheme
based on classical methods. We validate NeuSA on canonical benchmarks for lin-
ear and nonlinear wave equations, demonstrating strong performance as compared
to other architectures, with faster convergence, improved temporal consistency
and superior predictive accuracy. Code and pretrained models are available in
https://github.com/arthur-bizzi/neusa.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces a new method in the category of physics informed neural network (PINN) inspired by neural ode and spectral methods that can handle the causality problem faced by traditional PINNs. Instead of implementing the physical loss globally with time as an input variable, this method constructs a physical loss for each discretized step in time evolution, and expresses the result as an integral of time steps. The spatial part of the solution function is treated as a linear superposition of basis functions. In this case, Fourier basis are chosen as basis functions. Networks are applied to learn time-dependent coefficients at discretized time steps. This method shows better performance on three equations in comparison with other PINN approaches, and can generalize well to future steps unseen during training.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
	
1. The proposed method shows good extrapolation ability to unseen steps in the future, indicating that the model learns real "physics".
2. A good convergence speed is achieved by this method, in comparison with other methods mentioned in this paper.
3. The causality problem is solved with an approach like Neural ODE.
	
Weaknesses:
	
1. Some parts of this paper on boundary conditions and basis functions are confusing and inconsistent. A clarification is required. Please see question section for the most concerning points for the reviewer. 
2. A Runge-Kutta method is required for integrating the steps and obtain the result, which might make the inference slow.

QUESTIONS:
1. Are the basis functions in use fixed to be Fourier basis, or trained as networks? In lines 132-133 of main text, the paper indicates that the basis is "initialized" as Fourier basis. In lines 151-152 of main text, the paper also mentions that the basis is "trained". However, in equations 9 and 10, the dependency on trained parameters $\theta$ are not present in the basis functions $\bf{b(x)}$, indicating that those functions are not trained. In equations 25 and 26 in appendix B.1.2, the derivatives of exponential functions are calculated, also indicating that the basis functions are fixed to be Fourier. Can authors give a consistent description on basis functions?
2. The description on boundary condition fulfillment is confusing. In lines 132-133 of main text, the paper proposes to use basis functions to fulfill boundary conditions. However, boundary conditions are not satisfied with the choice of basis functions. For example, non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions should be satisfied by adding a specific term in the format of solution, so that the boundary conditions are homogenized. Can authors explain more on how boundary conditions are satisfied by the choice of basis functions?
3. In equation 11 of main text, a wave equation is shown to be solved in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, 2]$, which means in spatial dimensions, the domain expands to infinity. However, figure 8 of appendix, the spatial solution domain is shown to be $[-2, 2]^2$. If the spatial domain is extended to infinity, then the boundary condition is set to be 0 at infinity. If the spatial domain is now $[-2, 2]^2$, then what is the boundary condition imposed here?

LIMITATIONS:
The limitations of this work are discussed in the paper.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces NeuSA (Neuro-Spectral Architectures), a new class of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) designed to solve complex partial differential equations (PDEs), including time-dependent, nonlinear, and non-homogeneous types. Traditional MLP-based PINNs often struggle with spectral bias (favoring low frequencies) and causality violations. NeuSA overcomes these issues by:

- Projecting PDEs onto a spectral basis, capturing high-frequency dynamics.
- Integrating with Neural ODEs, which preserve temporal causality.
- Using classical spectral methods for initialization, starting training closer to the target solution.

NeuSA demonstrates superior performance on various benchmark PDEs (e.g., wave equation, Burgers’ equation, sine-Gordon equation), achieving higher accuracy, faster convergence, and better temporal consistency than other PINNs.

The architecture naturally satisfies boundary and initial conditions without extra training cost. Future work may extend NeuSA to more complex PDEs like Navier-Stokes and explore alternative spectral bases and numerical solvers to further improve performance and generalization.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
the spectral formulation, alongside the introduction of NeuralODE, seem to increase the accuracy in standard benchmark and preserve causality.

Weaknesses: 
NeuSA has not been tested in extreme case scenarios (very high dimensions, complex dynamics, etc.). It is therefore complex to assess NeuSA position within the vast PINN panorama.

QUESTIONS:
The authors should justify their claims by testing NeuSa in more complex setups and on a wider variety of PDEs (high dimensions, complex transient dynamics, etc.)

LIMITATIONS:
The major limitation of this work is the lack of an extended benchmark that would convince the readership of the claimed advantages of NeuSA. These kinds of benchmarks are nowadays necessary in order to position a new PINN version within the wide PINN's community.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper introduces NeuSA (Neurospectral Architectures) as a novel class of PINN architecture. This class satisfies by construction the boundary/initial conditions of the PDE, which alleviates one of the main training difficulty of PINNs, given by the imbalance in the loss components. Moreover, time dependency is resolved through continuous time integration based on a NeurODE formulation. The model also satisfies causality and uniqueness of the solution, which are two very interesting properties for a Physics-Informed model. The proposed NeuSA is validated on some benchmark PDEs and compared with state-of-the-art architectures.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The paper is in a very good format. In particular:
* The model is well presented, and the architectural choices are well motivated. Overall, the paper is well written.
* I find the combination of the PINN framework with the concept of NeurODE for the time integration very elegant.
* The model tackles by construction several long-standing issues of the PINN architecture.
* The achieved performance in training time is surprising, considering the time integration chosen.

On the other hand, there are some minor issues that concerns me:
* The PDEs considered for testing seem very suitable for the chosen architecture, due to the choice of a Fourier basis.
* The model only considers simple geometries, while I think the real challenge in imposing boundary conditions typically lies in more complex ones

QUESTIONS:
* I find it surprising that QRes outperforms NeuSA in the relative L2 Error over time (except far from the extrapolation region) in Figure 5, do you have some intuition on why this happens?
* I was wondering whether the authors tested also other training protocols (as quasi newton methods) which might enhance the performance of the Model in the training region. 
* Related to the question above, I would be curious to know (if the authors have tested it) what would be the extrapolation performance of the model when a NeuSA is trained to very good accuracy (say, around 10^-6 error) in the training region. Would the extrapolation error increase or stay limited?

LIMITATIONS:
The authors have addressed the limitations throughout the paper.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This study introduces NeuSA (Neuro-Spectral Architectures), which is an adaptation of the Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) architecture to the Physics Informed Neural Network (PINN) problem - that is, approximating the solution of a given PDE by using the PDE residual in the loss function. Inspired by the spectral methods for PDEs, NeuSA models the solution as a linear combination of spatial basis functions and uses a Neural ODE to learn the time dependence of the basis coefficients. Furthermore, while the basis functions are initialized to be the Fourier basis, these are also learned during training by the use of learnable orthogonal transforms. As the basis function expansion can be set up to satisfy both the initial and boundary conditions, the loss function only contains the PDE residual term. Experiments on challenging low dimensional systems show the strong performance of NeuSA compared to the baselines considered.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**

1. Strong performance on challenging problems

The problems considered in the paper such as wave propagation in heterogeneous media or 2D Burger's equation with shock development are all difficult problems that conventional PINNs tend to struggle on. However, the proposed approach returns accurate solutions for these problems with much less computational cost than its competitors.

2. Stable training dynamics due to improved architecture design

Due to the model formalism, NeuSA respects causality and does require multiple competing terms in its loss functions. This leads to faster and more stable training, as evidenced in the right panel of Figure 1.


3. Clear architectural motivations

The use of a Neural ODE-like architecture for the PINN problem is clearly motivated from the viewpoint of spectral methods for PDEs.


**Weaknesses**

1. Choosing the number of basis function elements

The number of Fourier basis elements used for training seems to be a key hyperparameter for this method, as it affects multiple aspects of training, including: 1) the maximum spatial frequency that the model output can have, 2) the computational cost of the model ($n$ basis elements for each of the $d$ spatial dimensions will scale roughly as O($n^d$), even with the dimensionwise layers used in this study), 3) how well the initial condition is described by the model. As such, an experiment to gauge the model performance as a function of number of basis elements used would provide more insights about the proposed method.

2. Confusion about the speed of the algorithm

The authors claim that despite the slowness of the Neural ODE architecture, NeuSA can achieve comparable or faster training times than competing non-Neural ODE approaches, with one of the reasons being NeuSA being able to compute the loss with a single forward pass whereas conventional approaches requiring multiple passes. While the shorter training times reported in Table 1 and Table 2,3,4 (in the Supplementary) seem to support this claim, it needs to be noted that the total training steps for NeuSA were 10 (2D Wave, 1D Sine-Gordon) or 100 times lower (2D-Burgers) than its counterparts. While impressive, this does mean that the per-step training time of NeuSA is often much higher than the other approaches (for 2D Wave, x3.57-x9.40 times slower), and the above claim does not seem to hold.

Minor

3. While recently proposed, a method for solving PDE that draws from the spectral method does exist in the literature [1]. While that approach and the current paper differs quite a bit, it would be great if the authors could incorporate the reference in their discussions, especially as the reference considers non-Fourier basis functions as well.

- [1]: J. Choi et al. Spectral operator learning for parametric PDEs without data reliance. Comput. Methods Appl. Mech. Engrg. 420, 15 (2024).

QUESTIONS:
The first part of my questions are related to the points raised in the Weaknesses section above.

1. How does the algorithm performance and the computational overhead change with the number of basis elements? Is this a hyperparameter to be sweeped over, or do the authors have a strategy / heuristic to determine the appropriate number? 

2. Related to the previous question, the initial conditions for the PDE problems in the experiments are either well described with finite Fourier basis elements (2D Burgers': product of sines/cosines; 2D wave, 1D sine-Gordon: Gaussian, whose spectrum decays as $\exp(-k^2)$ with increasing wavenumber $k$.). When the initial condition becomes more difficult to describe with a truncated Fourier series (such as a square wave), does this method still retain its performance?

3. I am curious about the per-epoch training time for each of the models considered in the paper. Is my reasoning correct, or have I made a mistake?

Some more minor questions are as follows:

4. Neural ODEs tend to struggle especially for long time series data, as the gradient pathologies become increasingly likely to occur (due to all the recurrent computation that stacks up while propagating the ODE solver forward in time) and the cost of the algorithm also scales linearly with time. In the context of NeuSA, this situation would correspond to solving a PDE over a longer temporal domain. Is this observed in practice? 

5. The algorithm's initialization scheme relies on linearizing the PDE and doing some algebra to derive the Fourier multipliers. Was this done manually or automatized, for example, by symbolic calculations?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper demonstrates several important strengths that justify its acceptance. Most notably, the proposed method exhibits strong extrapolation ability to unseen future steps, indicating that it successfully captures the underlying physical principles rather than merely fitting the training data. This is a critical feature for ensuring robustness and generalizability in scientific and engineering applications. In addition, the method achieves a faster convergence speed than other approaches discussed in the paper, highlighting its efficiency and practical utility, especially for large-scale problems where computational cost is significant. Furthermore, the authors effectively resolve the causality issue by employing a strategy inspired by Neural ODEs, thereby maintaining temporal consistency and physical plausibility in the learned dynamics. Equally important, the authors have carefully and appropriately addressed the reviewers’ questions, providing clarifications and revisions that further strengthen the manuscript. Taken together, these merits make the paper a valuable contribution, and I recommend acceptance.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
